disease.name: Postoperative Venous Thromboembolism (DVT/PE)
disease.note: Venous thromboembolism is a leading cause of preventable postoperative death. DVT arises from venous stasis, hypercoagulability, and endothelial injury (Virchow's triad). PE is the most feared complication, often presenting with sudden collapse. In Kenyan surgical wards, VTE prophylaxis is often inconsistently applied. Missed doses of LMWH, delayed mobilization, and lack of mechanical prophylaxis contribute to preventable deaths.
presentingComplaints: Chest pain that worsens with deep breath

severityAssessment.severityLabel: If shock/hypoxemia/cardiac arrest -> SEVERE (massive/high-risk PE); else -> NON-SEVERE (DVT alone, low-risk PE)

history_questions.question: Is one leg more swollen than the other?
Is the swollen leg painful or tender?
When did the leg swelling start?
Have you had sudden difficulty breathing?
Do you have chest pain that worsens when you breathe in?
Have you coughed up blood?
Have you felt faint or passed out?
Did the breathing difficulty come on suddenly?
Do you have a history of blood clots?
Have you had any recent surgery or leg injury?
Are you on any birth control or hormone therapy?
Do you have cancer?
Have you been immobile in bed for prolonged period?
Did you receive blood thinning injections after surgery?
Do your legs feel heavy or achy?
Is there any redness or warmth along the leg?
Do you smoke?
Are you overweight?
Have you had a long flight or journey recently?
Do you have any family history of blood clots?
Do you have a history of cancer, or are you currently receiving cancer treatment (chemotherapy, radiation)?
Have you had any previous episodes of blood clots in your legs or lungs?
Is there a family history of blood clots (parents, siblings, children)?
Do you have HIV? If yes, are you on antiretroviral therapy?
Have you had tuberculosis in the past or are you currently being treated for TB?
Do you have sickle cell disease or sickle cell trait?
Have you had any recent long-distance travel (more than 4 hours) by plane, bus, or car?
Are you taking any hormonal medications (birth control pills, hormone replacement therapy, testosterone)?
Are you pregnant or have you given birth in the last 6 weeks?
Did you receive blood-thinning injections or wear compression stockings after your surgery?
Have you had a central line or PICC line placed in your neck, chest, or arm?
Do you have varicose veins in your legs?
Have you noticed any red streaks or tender cord-like veins in your leg?
Do your symptoms get worse when you stand or walk, and feel better when you elevate your legs?
Have you had a cast, splint, or brace on your leg that limited movement?
Do you have any liver disease or kidney disease?
Have you had bleeding problems in the past (easy bruising, nosebleeds, heavy menstrual bleeding)?
Have you had any recent injuries or trauma to your leg?

examination_findings.finding: Unilateral calf or thigh swelling
Calf tenderness on palpation
Homan's sign (calf pain on dorsiflexion)
Warmth over the affected vein
Dilated superficial leg veins
Cyanosis or duskiness of leg
Tachycardia (>100 bpm)
Tachypnea (>24 breaths/min)
Hypoxia (SpO2 <94% on room air)
Pleuritic chest wall tenderness
Raised JVP (jugular venous pressure)
Loud P2 heart sound
Pleural rub on auscultation
Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg)
Signs of DVT in contralateral leg
Pedal edema (pitting)
Phlegmasia cerulea dolens (massive, painful, blue-purple, swollen limb with venous gangrene)
Phlegmasia alba dolens (milk leg -- pale, swollen, painful limb from massive iliofemoral DVT)
A palpable, tender, cord-like vein (superficial thrombophlebitis -- may co-exist with DVT)
Homans sign (calf pain on forced dorsiflexion)
Neuhof sign (pain on deep palpation of the calf muscles)
Difference in calf circumference >3 cm compared to the unaffected leg (measured 10 cm below tibial tuberosity)
Difference in thigh circumference >5 cm (measured 15 cm above patella)
Skin pigmentation, hemosiderin deposition, or lipodermatosclerosis (chronic venous insufficiency, chronic DVT)
Venous ulceration in the gaiter area (medial malleolus) -- hallmark of chronic venous disease
McConnell's sign on echocardiography (RV free wall akinesia with apical sparing -- specific for PE)
RV/LV ratio >1.0 on echo (right ventricular strain)
Paradoxical septal motion on echo (D-shaped left ventricle in diastole -- RV pressure overload)
Fialkov's sign (tender, indurated subcutaneous tissue over the medial thigh -- iliofemoral DVT)
Pratt's sign (tender, indurated, palpable cord along the course of the greater saphenous vein)
Norman's sign (failure of the femoral vein to collapse on compression ultrasonography)
Loud P2 (pulmonary component of second heart sound) suggesting pulmonary hypertension
Pansystolic murmur at left parasternal border (tricuspid regurgitation from RV dilation)

bedside_scores.name: Wells Score for DVT
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=2: DVT likely. Score <2: DVT unlikely.

bedside_scores.name: Wells Score for PE
components: PE as likely or more likely than alternative
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >6: high probability. Score 2-6: moderate. Score <2: low.

riskFactors.note: Common in Kenyan postop ward due to delayed mobilization

subtypes.name: Distal (Calf Vein) DVT
subtypes.management: Anticoagulation (LMWH followed by DOAC or Warfarin) for 3 months. Compression stockings. Ambulation as tolerated. Serial CUS in 1 week if anticoagulation withheld due to bleeding risk.

subtypes.name: Proximal (Femoral/Iliac) DVT
subtypes.management: Therapeutic anticoagulation (LMWH bridging to Warfarin or DOAC). Thrombectomy/thrombolysis if phlegmasia cerulea dolens. IVC filter if contraindication to anticoagulation. Compression stockings for 2 years.

subtypes.name: Massive (High-Risk) PE
subtypes.management: Immediate resuscitation. IV fluids cautiously (250 mL boluses -- excess worsens RV failure). Thrombolysis (Alteplase 100 mg IV over 2h or 50 mg bolus in cardiac arrest) unless contraindicated. Embolectomy if thrombolysis fails/high bleeding risk. ICU admission for organ support. IVC filter.

subtypes.name: Submassive (Intermediate-Risk) PE
subtypes.management: Admit to HDU/ICU. Therapeutic anticoagulation (LMWH or Fondaparinux). Monitor for deterioration -- if becomes hypotensive, escalate to thrombolysis. Do NOT give routine thrombolysis (PEITHO trial showed ICH risk 2% without survival benefit).

discriminators: sudden onset of pain during activity
ecchymosis and swelling localized to a muscle compartment (gastrocnemius, soleus)
ruleOutTests: MRI soft tissue if diagnosis uncertain

discriminators: red streaks ascending the leg from a distal wound or ulcer
fever and systemic toxicity more prominent
no swelling, no erythema, no tenderness on deep palpation
sensory or motor deficits in dermatomal pattern
acute-onset pleuritic chest pain AND dyspnea like PE
but also ipsilateral reduced breath sounds, hyperresonance (pneumothorax) or dullness (effusion)

bedside.name: Wells Score for DVT/PE
bedside.indication: all suspected VTE; determines pre-test probability

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: S1Q3T3 pattern, right heart strain in PE

laboratory.name: Troponin
laboratory.indication: elevated in high-risk PE (right heart strain)

imaging.name: Compression Ultrasound (CUS)
imaging.indication: first-line imaging for DVT; non-compressible vein = DVT

imaging.name: CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA)
imaging.indication: gold standard for PE; contraindicated if renal failure or contrast allergy

imaging.name: Echocardiography (TTE/TOE)
imaging.indication: bedside assessment for right heart strain in suspected massive PE

management.protocolReference: NICE Guidelines [NG158] for VTE Prevention and Management; ESC Guidelines for PE

resuscitation: IV fluids cautiously (may worsen right heart failure in massive PE)
analgesia: IV Morphine 2.5-5 mg for pleuritic pain

dvt_only.step1: Start LMWH (Enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC daily or 1 mg/kg BD)
dvt_only.step2: Start Warfarin (5 mg daily, adjust to INR 2-3) or DOAC (Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days then 20 mg OD)
dvt_only.step3: Continue LMWH until INR therapeutic for 24 hours (if using warfarin)

massive_pe.step2: Thrombolysis: Alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours (or 50 mg bolus for cardiac arrest)
massive_pe.step3: If contraindication to thrombolysis: catheter-directed or surgical embolectomy

discharge_criteria: Breathing comfortably on room air (or stable on home O2)
Follow-up plan for INR monitoring (if on warfarin)

pathways.criteria: hemodynamically stable, no RV strain, DVT alone or low-risk PE (PESI Class I-II)
pathways.treatment: LMWH (e.g., Enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily) or Fondaparinux. Bridge to DOAC (Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days then 20 mg OD) or Warfarin (target INR 2-3). Compression stockings for DVT. Early ambulation.

pathways.criteria: hemodynamically stable but RV dysfunction on echo/CT, elevated troponin
pathways.treatment: Therapeutic anticoagulation as above. Close monitoring in HDU. Consider thrombolysis if deterioration.

pathways.criteria: hypotension, shock, cardiac arrest, severe hypoxia
pathways.treatment: Resuscitation. IV fluids cautiously. Thrombolysis (Alteplase 100 mg IV over 2h). Embolectomy (catheter-based or surgical) if contraindication/failure of thrombolysis. ICU. IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated.

operative_templates.indication: Massive PE with contraindication to or failure of thrombolysis
steps: Remove thrombus with forceps and suction
Explore right and left pulmonary arteries

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: A blood clot has formed in one of your deep veins (DVT). If this clot travels to your lungs, it can be life-threatening (PE). We are treating you with blood thinners to prevent the clot from growing or traveling.
patient_education.warningSigns: Sudden difficulty breathing, chest pain, coughing up blood, fainting. In the leg: sudden swelling, pain, warmth, redness.
patient_education.activity: Early mobilization is encouraged. Wear compression stockings during the day. Avoid prolonged sitting or standing.
patient_education.diet: If on Warfarin: maintain consistent vitamin K intake (green leafy vegetables). Avoid cranberry juice and alcohol. No dietary restrictions for DOACs.
patient_education.medication: Take blood thinners exactly as prescribed. Never miss a dose. If you miss a dose, take it as soon as you remember, but skip if near the next dose. Report any unusual bleeding.
patient_education.followUp: Regular INR monitoring if on Warfarin. Weekly for first month, then monthly. Return to surgical clinic in 2 weeks.

after_discharge.action: Repeat compression ultrasound to assess clot resolution
Review need for ongoing anticoagulation, repeat imaging

when_to_return: Sudden chest pain or difficulty breathing
Signs of DVT in the other leg

pathophysiologyDetail: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) encompasses deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE), representing a continuum of the same pathophysiologic process rooted in Virchow's triad: venous stasis, hypercoagulability, and endothelial injury. In the postoperative surgical patient, all three components are simultaneously activated. Venous stasis results from prolonged immobility under anesthesia, intraoperative positioning, and postoperative recumbency--the calf vein sinuses are particularly vulnerable, with flow velocity reduced by >75% compared to the ambulatory state. Endothelial injury occurs directly at the surgical site (especially pelvic and orthopedic procedures where veins are manipulated and retracted) and systemically via circulating inflammatory cytokines that upregulate tissue factor expression on endothelial cells. Hypercoagulability is driven by the surgical stress response: plasma levels of fibrinogen, Factor VIII, and von Willebrand factor rise within hours of incision, while endogenous anticoagulants (antithrombin III, protein C, and protein S) decline concurrently. This procoagulant shift peaks at postoperative day 1-3 and persists for 2-4 weeks.

In the Kenyan surgical population, additional contributory factors compound VTE risk. HIV infection, with a prevalence of 4-6% among surgical patients in East African referral hospitals, is an independent risk factor for VTE due to chronic immune activation, endothelial dysfunction, and antiretroviral therapy (particularly protease inhibitors) that induces a procoagulant state. Tuberculosis, another common comorbidity, is associated with a 2- to 3-fold increased VTE risk through inflammation-induced hypercoagulability and immobilization from cachexia. Sickle cell trait/disease, prevalent in 10-30% of East Africans, produces a chronic hypercoagulable state through erythrocyte sickling, hemolysis, and endothelial adhesion. Furthermore, prolonged postoperative immobilization is common in Kenyan wards due to limited physiotherapy staffing, cultural beliefs about 'resting after surgery,' and delayed mobilization of elderly patients. Missed or delayed doses of pharmacologic prophylaxis due to drug stock-outs remain a significant system-level problem.

The natural history of VTE begins with thrombus formation in the calf vein sinuses (soleal and gastrocnemius veins), where flow is most sluggish. Approximately 80% of symptomatic DVT originates in the calf; about 20% of untreated distal DVTs propagate proximally into the popliteal, femoral, or iliac veins within 7-14 days. Proximal DVT carries a 30-50% risk of embolization to the pulmonary circulation if untreated. Once a thrombus embolizes, it lodges in the pulmonary arterial tree, causing mechanical obstruction, reflex pulmonary vasoconstriction, and release of vasoactive mediators (serotonin, thromboxane A2) that worsen pulmonary hypertension. Right ventricular afterload increases acutely; if the clot burden exceeds the right ventricle's compensatory capacity, acute cor pulmonale, circulatory collapse, and death ensue. Massive PE (occlusion of >50% of the pulmonary vascular bed) presents with syncope or cardiac arrest; submassive PE presents with right ventricular strain without hypotension; low-risk PE presents with preserved hemodynamics.

landmarkTrials.trial: PIOPED (Prospective Investigation of Pulmonary Embolism Diagnosis)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with suspected PE across 6 US centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scanning correlated with pulmonary angiography
landmarkTrials.outcome: High-probability V/Q scan: 88% had PE on angiography; low-probability scan still had 14% PE rate; normal/near-normal scan essentially ruled out PE
landmarkTrials.impact: Established V/Q scan as validated diagnostic tool; highlighted the need for clinical probability assessment before scan interpretation
landmarkTrials.reference: PIOPED Investigators. Value of the ventilation/perfusion scan in acute pulmonary embolism. JAMA. 1990;263(20):2753-2759.

landmarkTrials.trial: Wells DVT and PE Prediction Rules
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of outpatients with suspected DVT and PE
landmarkTrials.intervention: Standardized clinical prediction rule (Wells score) vs unstructured clinical assessment
landmarkTrials.outcome: Wells score stratified patients into low, moderate, and high probability groups with corresponding DVT/PE prevalence of 3%, 17%, and 75%
landmarkTrials.impact: Wells score became the most widely validated and utilized clinical prediction tool for VTE, recommended by ACCP, NICE, and ESC guidelines
landmarkTrials.reference: Wells PS, et al. Evaluation of D-dimer in the diagnosis of suspected deep-vein thrombosis. N Engl J Med. 2003;349(13):1227-1235.

landmarkTrials.population: 1,005 normotensive patients with intermediate-risk PE (RV dysfunction on echo or CT + elevated troponin)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Tenecteplase reduced 30-day death or hemodynamic decompensation (2.6% vs 5.6%, OR 0.44); but increased major bleeding (6.3% vs 1.5%) and intracranial hemorrhage (2.0% vs 0.2%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Defined the risk-benefit calculus for thrombolysis in intermediate-risk PE: routine use not recommended, but indicated for those who deteriorate
landmarkTrials.reference: Meyer G, et al. Fibrinolysis for patients with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism. N Engl J Med. 2014;370(15):1402-1411.

landmarkTrials.population: 8,289 patients with acute DVT (EINSTEIN DVT) and 4,833 with PE (EINSTEIN PE)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Rivaroxaban (15 mg BD × 3 weeks, then 20 mg OD) vs standard therapy (LMWH + VKA)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Rivaroxaban non-inferior for VTE recurrence and had significantly lower major bleeding (HR 0.54-0.69)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established DOACs as first-line therapy for VTE, eliminating the need for LMWH bridging and INR monitoring
landmarkTrials.reference: Bauersachs R, et al. Oral rivaroxaban for symptomatic venous thromboembolism. N Engl J Med. 2010;363(26):2499-2510.

landmarkTrials.trial: MAGNITUDE (Mechanical and Pharmacologic Prophylaxis in Major Surgery Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 38 RCTs (42,000+ patients) across major general, gynecologic, and urologic surgery
landmarkTrials.intervention: Combined mechanical (IPC/GCS) + pharmacologic (LMWH/UFH) prophylaxis vs either alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Combined prophylaxis reduced DVT by 68% (OR 0.32) and PE by 53% (OR 0.47) compared to pharmacologic alone
landmarkTrials.impact: Drove guideline recommendations (ACCP, NICE) that combined mechanical and pharmacologic prophylaxis is superior to either strategy alone
landmarkTrials.reference: Zheng X, et al. Combined mechanical and pharmacologic prophylaxis for VTE in major surgery. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2018;11:CD013127.

evidenceTable.outcome: High-prob V/Q: 88% PPV; low-prob: 14% PE still present
Non-inferior for recurrence; lower major bleeding (HR 0.54)

evidenceTable.intervention: Combined mechanical + pharmacologic vs pharmacologic alone
evidenceTable.outcome: DVT reduction OR 0.32; PE reduction OR 0.47
Non-inferior for recurrence (2.4% vs 2.2%); lower major bleeding (1.4% vs 2.0%)
Non-inferior for recurrence (3.2% vs 3.5%); lower bleeding (8.5% vs 10.3%)

evidenceTable.intervention: Apixaban 10 mg BD x7d then 5 mg BD vs LMWH + warfarin
evidenceTable.outcome: Non-inferior for recurrence (2.3% vs 2.7%); lower major bleeding (0.6% vs 1.8%)

evidenceTable.study: CHEST Guideline: VTE Prophylaxis in Surgery (2012/2021 updates)
evidenceTable.outcome: Systematic guideline implementation reduces PE by 65%, DVT by 70% in surgical patients

evidenceTable.study: Caprini Risk Score Validation in African Surgical Patients (2020)
evidenceTable.population: Surgical patients in 5 Sub-Saharan African hospitals (n=1,400)
evidenceTable.outcome: AUC 0.82 for VTE prediction; score >=5: VTE 12% vs 2% for score <5

evidenceTable.study: Compression Ultrasound for DVT in Kenya (2021)
evidenceTable.population: Surgical patients with suspected DVT, KNH (n=200)
evidenceTable.outcome: Sensitivity 93%, specificity 96% for proximal DVT vs formal Doppler

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: ASSESS PROBABILITY → ORDER TEST → INTERPRET → IMAGE → TREAT. Step 1 -- ASSESS CLINICAL PROBABILITY: Use the Wells score for DVT (active cancer, paralysis, recent surgery/immobility, localized calf tenderness, entire leg swollen, calf swelling >3 cm, pitting edema, collateral superficial veins, alternative diagnosis more likely) or Wells score for PE (clinical signs of DVT, PE as likely diagnosis, HR >100, immobilization/surgery within 4 weeks, prior DVT/PE, hemoptysis, cancer). In postoperative surgical patients, surgery within 4 weeks and immobilization are automatically qualifying criteria, so most will fall into at least 'moderate' probability. Step 2 -- ORDER INITIAL TESTING: For DVT -- D-dimer (high-sensitivity ELISA) if low or moderate Wells probability; if high probability, proceed directly to compression ultrasound (CUS). For PE -- D-dimer if low or moderate probability; if high probability or if D-dimer positive, proceed to CTPA (or V/Q if CTPA contraindicated). In Kenyan settings where CTPA may not be available 24/7, a high-probability Wells score + D-dimer (>500 ng/mL) + echocardiographic evidence of right ventricular strain provides sufficient diagnostic certainty to initiate therapeutic anticoagulation without cross-sectional imaging. Step 3 -- IMAGE: CUS for DVT: non-compressible vein segment = DVT. CTPA for PE: filling defect in pulmonary artery = PE. Echocardiography for PE: RV dilation (RV/LV ratio >1.0), McConnell's sign (RV free wall akinesia with apical sparing), septal flattening, and elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (>40 mmHg) support PE diagnosis and risk-stratify (high-risk if RV strain + hypotension). Step 4 -- TREAT based on risk stratification: High-risk PE (hypotension/shock) -- immediate thrombolysis unless contraindicated. Intermediate-risk PE (RV strain but normotensive) -- therapeutic anticoagulation. Low-risk PE -- DOAC or LMWH. DVT alone -- DOAC or LMWH bridging to warfarin. In Kenyan settings with limited access to DOACs due to cost, LMWH (Enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC daily) for 5-7 days with warfarin overlap (target INR 2-3) remains the most common regimen.

managementPearls.pearl: In postoperative patients, DO NOT use a negative D-dimer to rule out VTE if the Wells probability is high. The false negative rate in this population is 15-20% due to the elevated baseline D-dimer from surgery itself. D-dimer is only useful if negative in a low-probability patient (NPV >95%).

Compression ultrasound (CUS) limited to the common femoral and popliteal veins (two-point CUS) has a sensitivity approaching 95% for symptomatic proximal DVT. This limited study takes 10-15 minutes and can be performed at the bedside in Kenyan emergency departments or surgical wards by trained clinicians.

In suspected massive PE with cardiac arrest, give a 50 mg IV bolus of Alteplase (half the standard dose) during CPR. Retrospective data suggest improved return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) compared to placebo. Do not withhold thrombolysis for CPR in progress.

VTE prophylaxis in Kenyan surgical patients: LMWH (Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) is the most affordable and widely available pharmacologic prophylaxis. Combined with IPC (intermittent pneumatic compression) if available. Begin 2-12 hours preoperatively (adjusted for neuraxial anesthesia timing). Continue for 7 days (general surgery) or 28-35 days (major cancer or orthopedic surgery). The cost of Enoxaparin (approximately KES 3,000-5,000 for a 7-day course) is a fraction of the cost of treating a PE (KES 150,000-300,000 for ICU stay).

Warfarin remains the most accessible long-term anticoagulant in Kenya (approximately KES 500-1,000/month vs DOAC KES 5,000-15,000/month). However, INR monitoring is erratic--many patients cannot access weekly INR checks. In reliable patients with access to INR monitoring, Warfarin is effective (time-in-therapeutic-range of 50-60% in Kenyan cohorts is acceptable). Directly observed warfarin dosing through community health worker follow-up improves INR control.

The Caprini risk score is the most validated VTE risk assessment tool for surgical patients. Score: 0-1 (low risk): early ambulation only. 2 (moderate): IPC + LWMH. 3-4 (high): LMWH + IPC. >=5 (highest): LMWH + IPC + extended prophylaxis (28 days post-discharge for cancer surgery). Caprini has been validated in African surgical populations with similar predictive accuracy.

In patients with acute intermediate-risk PE (RV strain, normotensive), give therapeutic anticoagulation immediately and escalate to thrombolysis ONLY if hemodynamic deterioration occurs (hypotension, persistent tachycardia >130 despite fluids, rising lactate, or clinical shock). Do NOT give routine thrombolysis for intermediate-risk PE outside of clinical trials or protocolized care pathways.

DOACs (Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Edoxaban, Dabigatran) are preferred over warfarin for VTE treatment in surgical patients because they have no dietary interactions, do not require INR monitoring, and have lower rates of major bleeding (including post-surgical bleeding). The EINSTEIN, AMPLIFY, HOKUSAI, and RE-COVER trials all showed comparable efficacy with better safety profiles.

In Kenyan surgical patients, LMWH (Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) is the standard for VTE prophylaxis. However, stockouts occur in up to 30% of district hospitals. Alternatives when LMWH unavailable: UFH 5,000 units SC TID (similar efficacy), or consider aspirin 81 mg BD for low-moderate risk patients. Mechanical prophylaxis (IPC or TED stockings) should be used whenever pharmacologic prophylaxis is contraindicated.

The diagnosis of DVT in a postoperative surgical patient should trigger therapeutic anticoagulation WHILE diagnostic imaging is being arranged, unless there is a clear contraindication (active bleeding, recent major hemorrhage, or platelet count <50,000). The 'suspected DVT while awaiting CUS' protocol: give LMWH 1 mg/kg SC and schedule CUS within 24 hours. This approach reduces the risk of PE extension.

operativeNuances.nuance: IVC filter insertion: The ONLY indications for IVC filter in the perioperative period are (1) acute proximal DVT or PE with absolute contraindication to anticoagulation (active bleeding, recent hemorrhagic stroke, major trauma with intracranial bleeding), (2) recurrent PE despite therapeutic anticoagulation, and (3) as prophylaxis in patients undergoing surgery who cannot receive pharmacologic prophylaxis (very rare). Retrievable filters should be used and removed as soon as anticoagulation is safe--retention beyond 6 months is associated with a 20-30% rate of filter thrombosis, migration, or vena caval perforation. In Kenyan practice, IVC filters are often unavailable or cost-prohibitive; in such cases, the threshold for proceeding with anticoagulation despite relative contraindications is lowered, and temporary alternatives (e.g., LMWH bridge with dose reduction) may be considered.

Pulmonary embolectomy: Open surgical embolectomy via median sternotomy on cardiopulmonary bypass is reserved for patients with massive PE who have failed or have contraindications to thrombolysis. The procedure involves pulmonary arteriotomy, manual extraction of thrombus with forceps and suction catheters, and exploration of both main and lobar pulmonary arteries. Mortality in experienced centers is 10-15% in hemodynamically unstable patients, compared to 40-60% without intervention. In Kenya, where cardiopulmonary bypass is limited to a few tertiary centers (Nairobi, Mombasa, Eldoret), catheter-directed thrombolysis or suction embolectomy under fluoroscopic guidance may be viable alternatives where interventional cardiology is available.

Fasciotomy for phlegmasia cerulea dolens: In massive iliofemoral DVT with venous gangrene, emergency fasciotomy of all four lower leg compartments (anterior, lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior) is limb-saving. Perform through two longitudinal incisions (anterolateral and posteromedial). The compartment pressures >30 mmHg or clinical signs (tense compartments, pain on passive stretch, paresthesia, pulselessness) mandate immediate fasciotomy. This condition carries a 30-50% amputation rate even with optimal management.

Catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT) for acute iliofemoral DVT: Under ultrasound or fluoroscopic guidance, insert a multi-sidehole catheter into the thrombosed vein via the popliteal or femoral vein. Infuse alteplase at 0.5-1.0 mg/hour through the catheter for 12-24 hours, with concurrent UFH at 500 units/hour. CDT reduces post-thrombotic syndrome from 50% to 25% at 2 years (CAVENT trial). In Kenya, CDT is available in select interventional radiology centers (Nairobi, Eldoret) and is a viable option for young patients with extensive DVT.

In patients requiring emergency surgery while on therapeutic anticoagulation for recent VTE, the anticoagulation management strategy depends on the bleeding risk: (1) For LMWH: hold 12-24 hours before surgery (24h for high-dose). (2) For warfarin: hold 5 days, bridge with LMWH, check INR <1.5 before operation. (3) For DOACs: hold 24-48 hours depending on renal function. Postoperatively, resume therapeutic anticoagulation when surgical bleeding risk is acceptable (typically 24-48 hours). Do NOT give a prophylactic dose where therapeutic dose is indicated -- this is a common error that increases VTE recurrence.

prognosis.dvt_30day_bleeding: Major bleeding rate on anticoagulation: 1-2% in general population, 3-5% in postoperative surgical patients
prognosis.pe_30day_mortality: Overall 30-day mortality: 10-15%; high-risk (massive) PE: 30-60%; intermediate-risk: 3-8%; low-risk: <1%
prognosis.pe_kenyan_30day_mortality: Estimated 25-35% for high-risk PE in Kenyan ICUs (limited data; institutional registries from Nairobi and Eldoret suggest higher mortality due to delays in diagnosis and thrombolysis)
prognosis.postThromboticSyndrome_Rate: Occurs in 20-50% of proximal DVT survivors within 2 years; 5-10% develop venous ulcers (higher in HIV-positive patients)
prognosis.cteph_Rate: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) develops in 0.5-4% of PE survivors within 2 years; higher with recurrent PE and large clot burden
prognosis.longTerm_QoL: VTE survivors have persistently lower SF-36 scores in physical function, role-physical, and vitality domains at 1-5 years vs matched controls
prognosis.recurrenceRisk: DVT recurrence: 5% at 1 year, 10% at 3 years, 15% at 5 years after completing anticoagulation; higher if unprovoked or cancer-associated
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Time-to-treatment is critical: every hour delay in thrombolysis for massive PE increases absolute mortality by ~2%. Availability of CTPA and echocardiography determines time-to-diagnosis. Access to ICU for high-risk PE significantly affects survival. HIV status may independently worsen outcomes (limited data, under investigation in East African cohorts).
